texto: There is a feeling the body gives the mindof having missed something, a bedrock poverty, like fallingwithout the sense that you are passing through one world,that you could reach anotheranytime. Instead the realis crossing you,your body an arrivalyou know is false but can't outrun. And somewhere in betweenthese geese forever entering andthese spiders turning back,this astonishing delay, the everyday, takes place.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Jorie Graham: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “There is a feeling the body gives the mindof having missed something, a bedrock poverty, like fal…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, body, everyday pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de everyday e mind.

texto: Towards the end of the season it is not bad to have the body. To have experienced joy as the mere lifting of hunger is not to have known it less.
explicacao: Citação de Jorie Graham sobre temas ligados a felicidade. “Towards the end of the season it is not bad to have the body. To have experienced joy as the mere…” articula felicidade, body, hunger em registro de felicidade, com relevância para contextos como termino e hunger.

texto: and angle of vision, dust, gravity, solitude, and the part of the law which is the world's waitingand the part of the law which is my waiting,and the part which is my impatience—now; now?—though there are, there really arethings in the world, you must believe me.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Jorie Graham que provoca temas ligados a reflexao. Ao questionar “and angle of vision, dust, gravity, solitude, and the part of the law which is the world's waitin…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, poem, steering-wheel, com foco em steering-wheel.
